card.title: Why Elite Athletes Choke
card.teaser: The better you are, the worse pressure hits you. The brain science behind the yips is strange.
card.coverImagePrompt: A lone golfer standing over a three-foot putt at dusk, beads of sweat on the brow, crowd a blurred wall of colors behind, cinematic shallow focus, tension palpable

lessons.title: Greg Norman, 1996 Masters
lessons.body: Norman arrived at the final round of the Masters with a six-shot lead. By the back nine he was shaking. He shot a 78 — the worst final-round collapse in major championship history — and lost by five. Asked afterward what happened, he couldn't really say. His hands just wouldn't do what his hands had done a million times.

visual.imagePrompt: A close-up of trembling hands gripping a golf club, green fairway blurred behind, late-afternoon golden hour

lessons.title: Two systems, one shot
lessons.body: When you first learn a skill, your brain's explicit system runs the show — you consciously think through every step. Practice enough and the motion migrates to the implicit system in the basal ganglia and cerebellum, where it runs automatically and beautifully. Choking happens when pressure yanks the explicit system back into control of a skill that no longer needs it.

keyTerms.definition: The conscious, step-by-step control of movement used by beginners
The automatic motor control that experts rely on, running below conscious awareness

visual.imagePrompt: A stylized cross-section of a brain with two colored regions lighting up, labeled 'conscious' and 'automatic', scientific illustration

lessons.title: Paralysis by analysis
lessons.body: Psychologist Sian Beilock calls this 'explicit monitoring.' When the stakes rise, athletes start thinking about the mechanics — 'keep my left elbow straight, slow backswing, head down.' But those instructions are what you tell a novice. For an expert, micromanaging a skill you've automated is like trying to climb stairs while consciously directing each foot. You trip.

visual.imagePrompt: Split-screen infographic: left side shows a smooth fluid swing motion, right side shows the same swing broken into fragmented joint angles

lessons.title: The lab test
lessons.body: Beilock's team had expert golfers putt under two conditions: once while paying attention to their stroke mechanics, once while counting random tones on a speaker. The tone-counters putted better. Distracting the explicit system actually freed the expert motion. Novices showed the opposite pattern — they needed conscious attention to do anything at all.
lessons.funFact: Baseball's 'yips' have a name because the condition is so specific: established pros suddenly unable to throw the ball eighty feet to first base. Second basemen are hit hardest.

visual.imagePrompt: A laboratory with a golfer mid-putt wearing an EEG cap, researcher holding a clipboard behind translucent data graphs

lessons.title: How pros trick themselves back
lessons.body: The remedies are counterintuitive. Humming a single note, counting backwards, or focusing on a far-off target all occupy the explicit system enough that the automatic one can take over. Tiger Woods used to hum. Serena Williams bounced the ball a fixed number of times. The trick is giving your thinking brain just enough to do — so it stops meddling with the body's job.

quiz.question: Under pressure, choking happens because which system hijacks the other?
quiz.explanation: Pressure drags the conscious explicit system back into control of a motor skill that has been successfully automated.

quiz.question: In Sian Beilock's putting experiment, which expert group putted better?
options: Those verbally coached during the putt
quiz.explanation: Distraction freed the implicit system — the counterintuitive result that has been replicated in multiple expert sports.

quiz.question: Which is a documented on-course choking remedy?
options: Listing every mechanic of the swing aloud
Humming or counting a simple tune
Staring directly at the ball for 30 seconds
quiz.explanation: Simple rhythmic distractions occupy the explicit system enough to let automatic motor control execute unmolested.

sources.title: Choke: What the Secrets of the Brain Reveal About Getting It Right When You Have To
